lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: There’s matter in these sighs, these profound heaves:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: You must translate: ’tis fit we understand them.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Where is your son?

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Bestow this place on us a little while.

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Ah, mine good lord, what have I seen to-night!

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: What, Gertrude? How does Hamlet?

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Mad as the sea and wind, when both contend

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Which is the mightier: in his lawless fit,

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Behind the arras hearing something stir,

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Whips out his rapier, cries, “A rat, a rat!”

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: And, in this brainish apprehension, kills

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: The unseen good old man.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: O heavy deed!

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: It had been so with us, had we been there:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: His liberty is full of threats to all;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To you yourself, to us, to every one.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Alas, how shall this bloody deed be answer’d?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: It will be laid to us, whose providence

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Should have kept short, restrain’d and out of haunt,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: This mad young man: but so much was our love,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: We would not understand what was most fit;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: But, like the owner of a foul disease,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To keep it from divulging, let it feed

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Even on the pith of Life. Where is he gone?

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: To draw apart the body he hath kill’d:

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: O’er whom his very madness, like some ore

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Among a mineral of metals base,

lines.speaker: QUEEN
lines.text: Shows itself pure; he weeps for what is done.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: O Gertrude, come away!

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The sun no sooner shall the mountains touch,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: But we will ship him hence: and this vile deed

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: We must, with all our majesty and skill,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Both countenance and excuse. Ho, Guildenstern!

stage_directions.text: Enter King, Queen, Rosencrantz, and Guildenstern.

Friends both, go join you with some further aid: Hamlet in madness hath Polonius slain, And from his mother’s closet hath he dragg’d him: Go seek him out; speak fair, and bring the body Into the chapel. I pray you, haste in this. Exeunt Rosencrantz and Guildenstern. Come, Gertrude, we’ll call up our wisest friends; And let them know, both what we mean to do, And what’s untimely done. So haply slander—2 Whose whisper o’er the world’s diameter, As level as the canon to his blank, Transports his poison’d shot, may miss our name, And hit the woundless air. O, come away! My soul is full of discord and dismay. Exeunt.

glosses.phrase: Mad as the sea and wind
glosses.definition: raging as sea and wind together
a rat, a rat! (Hamlet's cry at Polonius)

annotations.title: Gertrude's equivocal loyalty
annotations.body: The scene opens with Gertrude reporting to Claudius in terms that protect Hamlet: his madness is 'as the sea and wind,' the killing of Polonius a 'brainish apprehension.' She does not reveal what Hamlet has told her — that Claudius murdered King Hamlet — and she does not tell the King that Hamlet is only feigning madness. Gertrude has always been one of the play's critical puzzles: is she complicit, merely survivalist, or confusedly loving both her son and her husband? This short scene contains her most decisive act of protection. Carolyn Heilbrun's influential reading argues that critics systematically underestimate Gertrude, who here emerges as the play's most skillful diplomat.
sources: Carolyn Heilbrun, 'The Character of Hamlet's Mother' (1957)

annotations.title: Claudius's political instincts
annotations.body: Claudius's immediate concern after hearing of Polonius's death is not grief but damage control: 'to us whose providence / Should have kept short, restrain'd and out of haunt / This mad young man.' His phrasing reveals the political mind — the killing reflects poorly on his management, and the King begins already to plan the concealment. By the end of his twenty lines he has arranged for the body's disposal, anticipated the slander to come, and resolved to accelerate the England voyage. This is Claudius at his most functional: not the tortured penitent of 3.3 but the professional sovereign managing a crisis. Shakespeare lets us see the King's competence precisely so we can measure its moral hollowness.

trials.prompt: What does Gertrude withhold from Claudius in her report of the closet scene?
options: That Hamlet's madness is feigned, and that he has accused Claudius of murdering King Hamlet.
That Hamlet saw the ghost during their conversation.
That Ophelia is wandering mad in the castle.

trials.prompt: What is Claudius's chief concern after learning of Polonius's death?
options: Concealing the crime and accelerating Hamlet's voyage to England.
Offering consolation to Laertes in France.
Consulting the priests about a proper burial.

trials.prompt: What does Gertrude's phrase 'Mad as the sea and wind' accomplish in context?
options: It diagnoses Hamlet as genuinely deranged, reinforcing Polonius's earlier theory.
It is a poetic evasion that protects Hamlet while giving Claudius a plausible story.
It signals to Claudius that Gertrude has lost faith in her son.
It quotes a proverb about the dangers of sea travel.
